topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box arrived on my doorstep, a stark white rectangle against the worn welcome mat. My fingers, usually steady, trembled slightly as I lifted the lid. Inside, a cascade of pale pink roses, their petals impossibly perfect, lay nestled in crinkled tissue paper. There was no note. Just the flowers. I stared at them, the vibrant color feeling almost too loud in the quiet hallway. A strange tightness began to coil in my chest, a familiar, unwelcome constriction. I traced the edge of a petal with my thumb, a futile attempt to anchor myself against the sudden, overwhelming wave of feeling. Who sent this? And why, in silence?

[story 2]

I found them tucked into the worn spine of my favorite book, a gesture so deliberate it felt almost theatrical. When I pulled them out, the scent—a heady mix of sweet perfume and damp earth—hit me immediately, making my eyes water. I held the bouquet loosely, my grip surprisingly weak. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. Every nerve ending seemed to be humming, overloaded by this unexpected display. I wanted to look for an explanation, a clue, anything to make sense of the silent offering, but the lack of context left me adrift in a sea of unanswered questions, my shoulders hunching inward as if trying to shield myself from the intensity.

[story 3]

It was Tuesday morning, and there, sitting on my desk, were sunflowers—big, brazen, and aggressively cheerful. I picked them up, and the weight of the stems felt heavy, grounding me in a sudden, dizzying sense of being scrutinized. I didn't look for a card; I didn't need one. The sheer volume of the yellow, the unapologetic brightness, was too much to process all at once. A prickling sensation rose along my neck. I sat back in my chair, my hands clenching into fists beneath the desk, trying to regulate the rapid flutter in my stomach. This unsolicited attention felt like a spotlight, and I desperately wished the light would just fade.
